definition: An influence that can change an object's speed, direction or shape. Force is described by its magnitude, direction and point of application, and is measured in newtons (N).
The unit of force (N), named after Isaac Newton. 1 N is approximately the force required to hold a small apple (100 g) against gravity.
The force that Earth's gravity exerts on an object – always directed straight downward towards Earth's centre. Calculated as F = m × g, where g ≈ 10 N/kg.
Indicates how much matter an object contains, measured in kilograms (kg). Mass is the same regardless of where in the universe the object is located – unlike weight.
The single force that replaces several forces acting on the same object. If the resultant is zero, the forces are in equilibrium and the object does not accelerate.
An object's resistance to changing its motion. An object at rest tends to stay at rest; an object in motion tends to continue moving in a straight line at constant speed (Newton's first law).
The change in velocity per unit time. Occurs when a net force acts on an object. Calculated using F = m · a (Newton's second law); measured in m/s².
Motion under the influence of gravity alone, without air resistance. All objects fall at the same rate regardless of mass (Galileo), with the gravitational acceleration g ≈ 10 m/s².

A braking force that arises when two surfaces slide against each other. Depends on the roughness of the surfaces and the weight of the object. Rolling friction is considerably less than sliding friction.

The point where the entire mass of an object can be thought of as concentrated. Gravity acts through the centre of gravity. Objects topple when the vertical line through the centre of gravity falls outside the base of support.
A rigid bar that rotates about a fixed pivot point and is used to save effort. The principle behind crowbars, wheelbarrows and scissors. Force × force arm = load × load arm.
Force per unit area, P = F / A, measured in pascals (Pa). The same force produces higher pressure when it acts on a small area – that is why a nail is sharp and snowshoes are wide.
